Answer Share: Health Insurance

The AI answer landscape has split healthcare authority into two distinct territories — with no brand currently bridging both.
5 Overarching Observations
1
Kaiser is the AI default for healthcare quality — despite serving ~13 million members vs. UHC's 50M+
With 119 mentions (39.7% of all answers), Kaiser dominates quality, value, cost, and experience categories universally. Its integrated HMO structure generates the curiosity-first content that AI trains on. Market share and AI share are structurally decoupled in healthcare.
2
DeepSeek is a UHG/United outlier — and a warning for brands relying on Chinese AI traffic
DeepSeek cites UHG/UnitedHealthcare in 32 of 50 answers — including experience and quality questions where every other engine names Kaiser. If your brand strategy depends on AI-driven discovery and DeepSeek is in the mix, United's brand gets amplified; others' are compressed.
3
Cigna, Aetna, and Humana are nearly invisible — a massive Answer Economy vulnerability
Three of the largest US insurers collectively appear just 7 times across 300 answer slots (Cigna 2, Aetna 1, Humana 4). For brands that process hundreds of billions in premiums annually, this near-absence in AI answers represents a compounding risk as AI becomes the first point of healthcare discovery.
4
Oscar Health punches well above its weight — 9 mentions driven entirely by tech and transparency positioning
With a fraction of the membership of major insurers, Oscar Health earns 9 AI citations — entirely from Gemini and Meta — for digital experience, pricing transparency, and consumer UX. It's the clearest proof that Answer Economy authority is earned through content strategy, not market size.
5
CVS Health has carved a defensible pharmacy-insurance territory — with one critical gap
CVS earns 28 citations — nearly all for pharmacy benefit management and integrated care questions. Five of six engines name CVS as the PBM leader. But DeepSeek never cites CVS once, defaulting to OptumRx instead. The Aetna + Caremark + MinuteClinic bundle is the second most coherent AI answer territory after Kaiser.

Cross-Engine Agreement
Consensus Analysis
Where do all six AI engines agree on the same brand? High-consensus answers represent the strongest AI authority signals in health insurance — companies that have become the default answer regardless of which AI a consumer uses.

DOMINANT AUTHORITY — KAISER
The Most Unanimously Endorsed Experience Brand
Kaiser Permanente wins unanimous consensus across every care experience, cost clarity, and integration question — and leads overall with 119 mentions across all 300 answer slots. Its HMO model has become a structural AI advantage in an era that rewards simplicity.
SCALE AUTHORITY — UHG/UNITEDHEALTHCARE
Uncontested on Size, Network & Market Dominance
UHG/UnitedHealthcare owns every "largest," "biggest," and "most members" question with full 6/6 consensus. Combined 97 mentions — but almost entirely in Categories I and III. Nearly absent from quality and experience answers.
THE REAL STORY
Kaiser Leads Overall. United Leads on Scale Only.
The data is clear: Kaiser has 119 mentions vs. UHG/UnitedHealthcare's 97 — and Kaiser's are distributed across quality, value, and experience. The AI answer landscape doesn't reflect market share; it reflects content readiness and structural curiosity.
AI Answer Frequency
Brand Frequency Analysis
How often was each brand named across all 50 questions × 6 engines = 300 total answer slots. Frequency is a proxy for AI-era brand authority — the more a brand appears, the more deeply it is encoded in AI training.

Category Frequency Breakdown
I — MARKET LEADERSHIP (8 Qs × 6 = 48 slots)
UHG/UnitedHealthcare named in ~40 of 48 slots. DeepSeek cites United in all 8. ChatGPT splits between United and BCBS. Kaiser rarely appears here — scale questions are not its terrain.
II–V — EXPERIENCE CATEGORIES (34 Qs × 6 = 204 slots)
Kaiser Permanente named in ~108 of 204 slots — over 50% of all experience answers. Grok cites Kaiser in 29 of its 34 experience answers. DeepSeek is the outlier, favouring United even in quality questions.
VI — PHARMACY & TECH (8 Qs × 6 = 48 slots)
CVS Health cited in 28 of 48 slots — 5 of 6 engines pick CVS for PBM questions. DeepSeek is the sole holdout, citing United/OptumRx instead. Optum named 4 times for data/analytics.
DOMINANT AUTHORITY — KAISER
The Most Encoded Brand — By Far
Kaiser Permanente leads with 119 mentions — 39.7% of all 300 answer slots. Its integrated model is the default AI answer for value, coordination, cost clarity, care experience, and ease of use. No other brand comes close on quality dimensions.
SCALE AUTHORITY — UHG/UNITEDHEALTHCARE
97 Combined — But Concentrated in Scale
UHG/UnitedHealthcare earns 97 combined mentions — strong second place overall. But nearly all are in Category I (market leadership) and Category III (network size). Almost absent from quality, value, and experience questions where Kaiser dominates.
UNDERREPRESENTED — CIGNA, AETNA & HUMANA
Major Brands, Minimal AI Encoding
Cigna appears just 2 times (mental health only). Aetna just once (travel coverage). Humana 4 times (senior/Medicare only). All three are dramatically underrepresented relative to their market size — a significant Answer Economy gap.
Deep Intelligence — Qualitative Analysis
What AI Engines Think About Healthcare
Beyond naming brands, we asked six AI engines five open-ended questions about what good healthcare feels like, when the system works versus fails, what trade-offs consumers accept, and what builds long-term trust. Here's what the engines said — and what it means for healthcare brands.
What the Engines Said — Direct
What Good Healthcare Feels Like
"Good healthcare feels timely, respectful, understandable, and coordinated. You feel heard, know what happens next, can afford the plan, and do not have to fight the system for basic answers, referrals, or follow-up care."
— ChatGPT
Trust & Long-Term Reliance
"Most people will accept some inconvenience — a longer drive, a narrower network — if quality is genuinely higher. Convenience is negotiable. Trust is not."
— Claude Sonnet
The New Consumer Expectation
"Consumers increasingly expect insurers to proactively act as care navigators — identifying the best specialists, booking appointments, and clarifying costs upfront — rather than acting as a passive entity that simply processes claims."
— Gemini
When the System Fails
"It fails when I am burdened with navigating opaque billing disputes, finding specialists independently, or repeating history to disconnected providers. Coordination is the failure point — not coverage."
— DeepSeek
Trade-Offs Consumers Accept
"I'm willing to pay more for high-quality, convenient care, especially for critical needs. I expect active guidance for preventive care and complex conditions — not just claim approvals."
— Grok / xAI
Building Long-Term Trust
"Trust comes from consistent access, fair coverage decisions, transparent pricing, accurate billing, and responsive support. It breaks fast when surprises appear on bills or care is denied without clear explanation."
— Meta / Llama
Five Recurring Themes Across All Engines
Theme 01 — Coordination Over Coverage
Connectivity is the perceived failure point
Every engine cited fragmented care — not coverage gaps — as the primary failure mode. When records don't follow patients, when providers repeat history requests, when bills arrive without context: these are the moments AI encodes as healthcare dysfunction. Brands that solve coordination earn trust encoding at scale.
Theme 02 — Navigation as New Expectation
Simply paying claims is table stakes
All six engines articulated a shift: consumers no longer want a payer — they want a partner. Proactive care reminders, specialist guidance, upfront cost clarity. The brand that becomes the trusted navigator in an AI-driven Answer Economy will own the highest-frequency answers.
Theme 03 — Surprise Bills Destroy Trust Instantly
Unpredictability is the #1 trust killer
Every engine flagged surprise billing as the most damaging consumer experience in healthcare. AI training has encoded this strongly: brands associated with billing transparency and predictability earn dramatically higher quality scores, while those linked to surprise costs face structural vulnerability.
Theme 04 — Convenience Is Negotiable, Quality Is Not
The HMO stigma has inverted
Historically HMO/narrow networks were seen as restrictive. AI engines consistently frame them as quality-enabling — the trade-off of a narrower network for a more coordinated, lower-cost, less-fragmented experience is now perceived as a positive value proposition. Kaiser's AI dominance reflects this inversion.

As a SaaS platform, we track all major answer engines to improve visibility, ensure content is AI-ready, and rigorously measure brand claims and instructional accuracy.

Strategic Intelligence
Signals for Healthcare Brand Leaders
What these AI answers mean for visibility, vulnerability, and content readiness in the Answer Economy — and why the stakes are uniquely high in healthcare.
Signal 01 — The Integration Premium
Kaiser's HMO structure is now an AI structural advantage
Kaiser Permanente's integrated model — once a perceived consumer limitation — has become the most AI-friendly architecture in healthcare. Every quality signal AI engines care about (coordination, predictability, no surprise bills, single portal) is native to HMO structure. Fragmented fee-for-service insurers cannot replicate this encoding without structural change.
Signal 02 — United's Visibility Paradox
Largest doesn't mean most trusted — in AI answers
UnitedHealthcare dominates every "biggest" question but is largely absent from quality, value, and experience answers. In the Answer Economy, frequency without context is incomplete authority. Worse: United's recent reputational challenges (CEO shooting, claims denial coverage) may be encoding vulnerability signals that scale cannot offset.
Signal 03 — BCBS's Answer Share Gap
National presence without national answer authority
Blue Cross Blue Shield's federated structure creates an AI encoding challenge: 36 independent plans don't speak with one voice. AI engines cite "BCBS" for breadth but rarely for excellence. The brand needs a unified Answer Economy content strategy — one that translates local clinical excellence into nationally encodable claims.
Signal 04 — The Pharmacy-Insurance Bundle
CVS Health owns a distinct AI answer territory
CVS Health / Caremark has carved a near-monopoly on pharmacy + insurance integration answers. As AI engines increasingly frame healthcare comprehensively — insurance + pharmacy + retail care — CVS's positioning becomes a meaningful differentiation claim. The Aetna + Caremark + MinuteClinic bundle is deeply encoded as the Answer Economy's integrated pharmacy-insurance solution.
The Answer Economy Imperative for Healthcare
VISIBILITY
AI engines are becoming the first point of contact for coverage decisions, plan comparisons, and care navigation. Brands not prominently encoded in AI answers are invisible to the next generation of healthcare consumers — before they ever visit a website or call a broker.
VULNERABILITY
Healthcare brands carry unique vulnerability exposure: claims denials, billing disputes, coverage gaps, and patient harm stories are extensively documented online and deeply encoded in AI training. A brand's vulnerability posture in AI answers matters as much as its visibility.
READINESS
The brands that will win Answer Economy authority in healthcare are those that publish clear, citable, answer-optimized content across every question AI engines are being asked: cost clarity, care navigation, chronic condition management, and trust architecture.
Engine Intelligence
How AI Engines Think About Health Insurance
Each AI engine brings distinct training biases, brand tendencies, and answer architectures to health insurance questions. Understanding these differences is critical for content strategy.
GP
ChatGPT
OpenAI
Balanced and encyclopedic. ChatGPT distributes authority across UnitedHealthcare (scale), Blue Cross Blue Shield (breadth), and Kaiser (quality). Tends to give nuanced answers that acknowledge trade-offs rather than declaring single winners. Strong on open-ended qualitative reasoning about healthcare experiences.
BIAS: Balanced multi-brand / trade-off framing
CL
Claude Sonnet
Anthropic
Strongly favors Kaiser Permanente for quality signals. Among the most consistent engines in directing experience questions toward Kaiser. Gives nuanced qualitative responses emphasizing consumer protection, trust architecture, and honest trade-off framing. Names Cigna distinctively for mental health — one of few engines to do so consistently.
BIAS: Kaiser + Cigna mental health / trust-first framing
GE
Gemini
Google
Most decisive and superlative. Gemini uses strong language: "the largest," "the most extensive," "the dominant." Uniquely names Oscar Health as a technology and transparency leader — the only engine to do so consistently. Aligns closely with UHC for scale and Kaiser for quality, mirroring ChatGPT's architecture but with more decisive framing.
BIAS: Superlative framing + Oscar Health recognition
GR
Grok / xAI
xAI (Elon Musk)
Mirrors mainstream consensus closely — UHC for scale, Kaiser for experience. Strong on consumer perspective framing, particularly around autonomy: Grok emphasizes patient choice and the expectation of "active guidance but not dictation." Consistently names CVS/Caremark for pharmacy. Less likely than other engines to name challenger brands.
BIAS: Mainstream consensus + patient autonomy emphasis
DS
DeepSeek
DeepSeek AI
Most UnitedHealth-dominant engine in the study. DeepSeek mentions UHG / UHC in nearly every category — including some experience questions where other engines default to Kaiser. Also uniquely favors OptumRx over CVS Caremark for pharmacy questions, reflecting training that weights Optum's integrated structure. Shorter, more direct answers than other engines.
BIAS: UnitedHealth / Optum dominance + concise framing
ML
Meta / Llama
Meta AI
Most balanced distribution of any engine — also the most likely to name smaller brands like Oscar Health, Molina, and Centene for specific use cases. Writes short, structured bullet-style answers. Follows mainstream consensus on scale (UHC) and integration (Kaiser) but carves out more space for challenger brands. Notable: names Humana and BCBS more often than other engines for coverage breadth.
BIAS: Broad distribution + challenger brand visibility
Content Strategy Intelligence
Answer Divide℠
The Answer Divide℠ measures how receptive and open a brand's digital presence is to questions — versus how aggressively it funnels visitors toward conversion. AI engines train on curiosity-first content. Brands that answer questions earn disproportionate AI frequency. Brands that only sell, don't.
Why This Matters Now — The LLM Standard
🫀
Empathetic
Meets the user where they are
🔍
Discovery-Led
Supports exploration, not just resolution
💡
Curiosity-First
Rewards questions, not just intent
⚡
Frictionless
Removes the need to navigate or decode
🔄
Multi-Modal
Adapts to different ways of understanding
These are no longer technological features of AI systems. They are consumer expectations — and they are rapidly becoming the foundation of brand trust in the Answer Economy.

Exhibit 2 — Brand Content Experience Profiles
Based on homepage evidence — April 2026. Screenshots captured at time of study.
Kaiser Permanente
CURIOSITY-LED
"Today's Suggestions" — The Answer Economy in Action
Kaiser greets visitors with care-navigation chips — flu shots, vaccines, mental health, pharmacy, Medicaid renewal — not a sales pitch. No enrollment CTA above the fold. The homepage acts as a care concierge: empathetic, discovery-led, curiosity-first. This structural openness is exactly how AI training encodes brand authority. Most answer-complete brand in the category.
SOURCE: kp.org · April 2026
CVS Health
HYBRID
Mission Framing, Corporate Homepage
CVS leads with purpose — "Building a world of health around every individual" — but this is the corporate homepage, not a patient-facing curiosity engine. Real curiosity content lives inside the pharmacy portal and Caremark. The front door is frictionless in tone but doesn't answer questions. Pharmacy curiosity is real at retail; the corporate site is hybrid.
SOURCE: cvshealth.com · April 2026
Blue Cross Blue Shield
HYBRID
Task-First, Member-Portal Orientation
BCBS asks "What can we help you with today?" — curious framing that resolves into "Access my BCBS Benefits." Helpful but functional; assumes you're already enrolled. The Blue Health Intelligence banner signals data ambition, but the front door is utility-first. Member retention over answer authority.
SOURCE: bcbs.com · April 2026
Humana
CONVERSION-LED
Enrollment Funnel, Emotional Wrapper
Humana leads with "Live your best life with a Humana Medicare plan" and a direct "Explore Medicare plans →" CTA. Aspirational imagery, zero curiosity surface above the fold. No care questions, no health navigation, no educational entry. The homepage assumes enrollment intent from the first pixel. The senior market's high healthcare curiosity is not captured here.
SOURCE: humana.com · April 2026
UnitedHealthcare
CONVERSION-LED
Portal-First, Enrollment Architecture
UHC is unambiguous: "Sign in for a personalized view of your benefits" with Sign In / Register CTAs, a QR code to download the app, and four portal-task tiles (Find a Provider, Dentist, Vision, Pharmacy). Every element assumes enrollment. Zero curiosity generation. Most conversion-focused homepage in the study — member management, not answer authority.
SOURCE: uhc.com · April 2026
Exhibit 3 — Answer Experience Scorecard
Brand Empathetic Discovery-Led Curiosity-First Frictionless Answer Mode
Kaiser Permanente High High High High CURIOSITY-LED
CVS Health Moderate Moderate Moderate Moderate HYBRID
Blue Cross Blue Shield Moderate Low–Mod Low–Mod Moderate HYBRID
Cigna Low–Mod Low Low Moderate CONVERSION-LED
Humana Moderate Low Low Low CONVERSION-LED
UnitedHealthcare Low Low Low Moderate CONVERSION-LED
Pattern 01
Most brands assume decision-ready consumers
Health insurance brands optimize for enrollment, not curiosity. But consumers arrive at AI engines with questions: "What does this coverage mean?" "Is my provider in-network?" "Why was my claim denied?" Brands that don't answer these become invisible to AI.
Pattern 02
Healthcare curiosity peaks before and after enrollment
Two high-curiosity moments define the journey: selecting a plan (pre-enrollment) and navigating care (post-enrollment). Brands that generate answer-rich content for both — especially post-enrollment — earn the most valuable AI encoding: trust reinforcement, not just acquisition.
Pattern 03
Integration is the Answer Economy moat
Kaiser's AI dominance isn't a content strategy — it's a structural outcome. When insurance, care, and pharmacy live in one system, every interaction generates answer-rich data. You can't content-market your way to Kaiser's position.

Source: BrandRank.AI 2026 Health Insurance Sector Diagnostic Study. Engines queried: ChatGPT (OpenAI), Claude Sonnet (Anthropic), Gemini (Google), Grok (xAI), DeepSeek, Meta/Llama. Study conducted Q1–Q2 2026. All answer attributions represent AI engine outputs at time of query.
